title: Tax Rules Renting Out Principal Residence Canada
metaDescription: Learn tax rules renting out principal residence Canada, including CRA deadlines, T776 line numbers, and provincial rules to avoid penalties and stay compliant.
content:
As a Canadian landlord, understanding the tax rules renting out principal residence Canada is crucial to avoid penalties and ensure compliance with the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA). When you rent out your principal residence, you are subject to various tax rules and regulations. In this article, we will delve into the key aspects of tax rules renting out principal residence Canada, including CRA deadlines, T776 line numbers, and provincial rules.

## Introduction to Tax Rules Renting Out Principal Residence Canada
The tax rules renting out principal residence Canada can be complex, especially for accidental landlords who are new to renting out their properties. The CRA considers your principal residence as a property that you or your family members have lived in at some point during the year. When you rent out your principal residence, you are required to report the rental income on your tax return and claim the corresponding expenses.

For example, let's say you rented out your principal residence in Toronto for $2,000 per month. You would need to report this income on your tax return and claim expenses such as mortgage interest, property taxes, and maintenance costs. You can claim these expenses on line 21200 of your T776 statement of real estate rentals.

## Understanding the Principal Residence Exemption
The principal residence exemption is a tax exemption that allows you to exclude the gain from the sale of your principal residence from your taxable income. However, when you rent out your principal residence, you may be subject to a deemed disposition, which means that the CRA considers you to have sold your property and immediately re-purchased it. This can result in a taxable gain, even if you haven't actually sold the property.

To avoid this, you can file a principal residence exemption election with the CRA. This election allows you to designate your property as your principal residence and exclude the gain from the sale of the property from your taxable income. You can learn more about the principal residence exemption and how to file the election in our article [Navigating the CRA Principal Residence Exemption: A Guide for Canadian Landlords](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/navigating-the-cra-principal-residence-exemption-a-guide-for-canadian-landlords).

## Tax Rules Renting Out Principal Residence Canada: CRA Deadlines
The CRA has specific deadlines for filing your tax return and reporting your rental income. The deadline for filing your tax return is April 30th of each year, and you are required to report your rental income on your T776 statement of real estate rentals. You can claim your expenses on line 21200 of your T776 statement, and you are also required to complete a T1135 foreign asset verification statement if you have foreign assets worth more than $100,000.

For example, let's say you rented out your principal residence in Vancouver for $1,500 per month. You would need to report this income on your tax return and claim expenses such as mortgage interest, property taxes, and maintenance costs. You would also need to complete a T1135 foreign asset verification statement if you have foreign assets worth more than $100,000.

## Tax Rules Renting Out Principal Residence Canada: Provincial Rules
In addition to the federal tax rules, there are also provincial rules that apply to renting out your principal residence. For example, in Ontario, you are required to charge and remit harmonized sales tax (HST) on short-term rentals, such as those rented through Airbnb. You can learn more about the tax rules for short-term rentals in our article [Airbnb Tax Rules: Canadian Landlords 2026](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/airbnb-tax-rules-canadian-landlords-2026).

In British Columbia, you are required to pay a speculation and vacancy tax on vacant or underutilized properties. This tax applies to properties that are not occupied by the owner or a tenant for more than six months of the year. You can learn more about the speculation and vacancy tax on the Government of British Columbia's website.

## CRA-Compliant Bookkeeping for Canadian Landlords
To ensure that you are meeting your tax obligations and taking advantage of all the deductions you are eligible for, it's essential to have a CRA-compliant bookkeeping system in place. This includes keeping accurate records of your rental income and expenses, as well as completing the necessary tax forms and statements. You can learn more about CRA-compliant bookkeeping for Canadian landlords in our article [CRA-Compliant Bookkeeping for Canadian Landlords: The Complete Guide](https://www.rentalops.ca/blog/cra-compliant-bookkeeping-canadian-landlords-complete-guide).

You can try RentalOps for free and see how it can help you stay organized and compliant with the CRA.

## Common Mistakes to Avoid
There are several common mistakes that Canadian landlords make when renting out their principal residence. These include:

* Failing to report rental income on their tax return
* Not claiming all eligible expenses on their T776 statement
* Not filing a principal residence exemption election with the CRA
* Not keeping accurate records of rental income and expenses
* Not completing the necessary tax forms and statements

These mistakes can result in penalties and interest charges, as well as missed opportunities for tax savings. By understanding the tax rules renting out principal residence Canada and having a CRA-compliant bookkeeping system in place, you can avoid these mistakes and ensure that you are meeting your tax obligations.

## Key Takeaways
Here are the key takeaways from this article:

* The tax rules renting out principal residence Canada can be complex, but understanding them is crucial to avoiding penalties and ensuring compliance with the CRA.
* You are required to report your rental income on your tax return and claim expenses on your T776 statement.
* You may be subject to a deemed disposition when you rent out your principal residence, but you can avoid this by filing a principal residence exemption election with the CRA.
* There are provincial rules that apply to renting out your principal residence, such as the speculation and vacancy tax in British Columbia.
* Having a CRA-compliant bookkeeping system in place is essential to meeting your tax obligations and taking advantage of all the deductions you are eligible for.

## Conclusion
In conclusion, the tax rules renting out principal residence Canada can be complex, but understanding them is crucial to avoiding penalties and ensuring compliance with the CRA. By having a CRA-compliant bookkeeping system in place and taking advantage of all the deductions you are eligible for, you can minimize your tax liability and ensure that you are meeting your tax obligations.
